title: Ordering, Single Active Consumer, Keys, and Partitioning · Mastery Check
summary: A production review compares near-miss TypeScript messaging decisions for ordering, single active consumer, keys, and partitioning and requires the smallest defensible ordering scope and partition strategy with changed-case evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for ordering scope and partition strategy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: State ordering scope across producer channel, exchange route, one queue, delivery, completion, redelivery, priority, and multiple consumers.
Partition related messages by stable business key while bounding skew, hot keys, resharding, and cross-key independence.
Use single active consumer when one active delivery stream is required and account for failover, prefetch, recovery, and throughput limits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.text: A quiz messaging team handles case 5191 in a multi-tenant case event backbone with three subscriber responsibilities. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to enforce partition, version, gap, and single-active evidence instead of claiming global order; keep every queue and subscriber responsibility explicitly named; then reuse one message identity with a different payload fingerprint. For RabbitMQ and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Ordering Throughput Tradeoff, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Strict global order can scale horizontally without coordination cost.
